---
title: "Project 2 — Power BI"
format: html
---

# Project 2 — Power BI

**Power BI**  
**Business Intelligence**  
**Visualisation**

Dashboard design, business storytelling, and performance visualisation using Power BI.

## Overview

This project focuses on business dashboard development and visual communication using Power BI. It involved presenting business performance data in a way that is clear, interactive, and suitable for decision-making. The dashboard was designed to highlight key business indicators such as sales, costs, net profit, and category performance, while also making the information visually accessible for a wider audience.

This project is important in my portfolio because it demonstrates my ability to move from raw business data toward organised visual insight. It also shows how data presentation plays a key role in helping people understand performance and identify patterns.

## Dashboard Screens

![](../images/powerbi-1.jpg){fig-alt="Power BI dashboard screen 1"}
![](../images/powerbi-2.jpg){fig-alt="Power BI dashboard screen 2"}

These dashboard screens show how business data can be summarised visually to support comparison, interpretation, and decision-making.

## Method

The dashboard was designed to summarise business performance across categories and support quick comparison of key measures. I focused on selecting the most meaningful indicators, organising them into a clear layout, and presenting trends in a visually readable way. This process strengthened my understanding of how dashboard structure affects interpretation.

## Python Example

The Python example below demonstrates a simple category-level comparison that supports dashboard interpretation and business reporting.

```{python}
#| echo: true
#| code-fold: true
#| fig-cap: "Example Python visualisation of category-level business performance"

import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

data = pd.DataFrame({
    "Category": ["Travel", "Construction", "Electronics", "Finance", "Retail"],
    "Net_Profit": [82, 76, -14, 58, 41]
})

plt.figure(figsize=(8, 5))
plt.bar(data["Category"], data["Net_Profit"])
plt.title("Net Profit by Category")
plt.xlabel("Category")
plt.ylabel("Net Profit")
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

This example shows how categories can be compared visually through executable code. It supports the wider project theme of turning business data into clear and useful insight.

## Key Learning

A key learning point from this project was that visualisation is not only about making data attractive. It is about helping users notice patterns, compare results, and make decisions more efficiently. I learned that the choice of chart, layout, and metric all influence how useful a dashboard becomes.

## Reflection

This project helped me appreciate the value of visual storytelling in business analysis. I learned that a dashboard is not just about displaying charts, but about organising information in a way that makes insight easier to recognise and interpret. It also showed me the importance of design choices, layout, and selecting the most useful metrics for a business audience.

Working on this project improved my confidence in balancing analytical content with presentation quality, which is an important skill in modern business and reporting environments.

## Skills Gained

Through this project, I developed stronger skills in Power BI, dashboard design, visual business reporting, and data presentation. I also improved my ability to identify useful business measures and think about what information decision-makers need most. In addition, the project strengthened my sense of layout, communication, and professional presentation. These are valuable skills for business intelligence, reporting, and analytical roles where visual clarity matters.
